title: Async functions always return a Promise
instruction:
The most important rule: any function marked `async` returns `Promise<T>`, even if you return a plain value. TypeScript enforces this and tracks what's inside the Promise.

See how TypeScript types async functions and how `await` unwraps the Promise.
successMessage: `await` unwraps the Promise — inside `displayUser`, `user` has type `{ id, name, role }`, not `Promise<...>`. TypeScript tracks this automatically.

title: Parallel async with Promise.all
instruction:
Running async operations sequentially when they're independent wastes time. `Promise.all` runs them in parallel and TypeScript correctly types the result as a tuple.

Fetch three independent resources at once instead of one by one.
expectedOutput: Carol: 3100 XP, badges: streak_30, speedster, all_done
successMessage: `Promise.all` returns a tuple — TypeScript knows `[name, score, badges]` are `[string, number, string[]]`. Three independent fetches run simultaneously instead of sequentially.

title: Handling errors properly in async code
instruction:
Async functions can fail. The two patterns — `try/catch` and `Promise.allSettled` — handle failures differently. `allSettled` is better when you want all results even if some fail.

Build a dashboard loader that handles partial failures gracefully.
successMessage: `Promise.allSettled` — unlike `Promise.all` — never rejects. It returns all results, fulfilled or rejected. The dashboard still loads even when one service is down.
